pages.title: The Carbon Cycle
pages.description: Learn about how carbon moves through our planet in a continuous cycle.
pages.explanation: Carbon is one of the most important elements on Earth! Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air through photosynthesis, storing carbon in their leaves, stems, and roots. When animals eat plants and breathe out, they release carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere. This natural exchange is called the carbon cycle - it keeps our planet balanced and healthy!

pages.title: What is Carbon Cycle?
pages.description: Discover how carbon moves through Earth's systems and connects all living things.
pages.explanation: The carbon cycle is the way carbon travels through our world. Carbon dioxide in the air is absorbed by plants during photosynthesis. When animals eat plants, carbon moves through the food web. Carbon connects every living thing on Earth - from tiny bacteria to giant whales to you!

pages.title: Oceans and Carbon
pages.description: Explore how our oceans help absorb carbon from the atmosphere.
pages.explanation: Our oceans are like giant carbon sponges! They absorb about 25% of the carbon dioxide we produce. Tiny ocean plants called phytoplankton use carbon dioxide just like land plants do. However, when oceans absorb too much CO2, they become more acidic, which can harm coral reefs and marine life.

pages.title: Forests: Earth's Lungs
pages.description: Learn why forests are so important for fighting climate change.
pages.explanation: Forests are often called the 'lungs of the Earth' because they breathe in carbon dioxide and breathe out oxygen. A single large tree can absorb up to 48 pounds of carbon dioxide per year! That's why protecting forests and planting new trees is so important for our planet's health.

pages.title: The Greenhouse Effect
pages.description: Understand how certain gases trap heat in our atmosphere.
pages.explanation: The greenhouse effect is like a blanket around Earth. Some gases in our atmosphere, called greenhouse gases, trap heat from the sun and keep our planet warm. This is natural and necessary for life! But when we add too many greenhouse gases by burning fossil fuels, the blanket gets too thick and Earth gets too warm.

pages.title: Melting Ice
pages.description: See how rising temperatures are affecting polar regions.
pages.explanation: As Earth gets warmer, the ice at the North and South Poles is melting faster than ever before. This is dangerous because ice reflects sunlight back into space, helping keep Earth cool. When ice melts, it also raises sea levels, which can flood coastal cities and change habitats for polar animals like polar bears and penguins.

pages.title: Extreme Weather
pages.description: Learn how climate change is making weather more unpredictable.
pages.explanation: Climate change is making our weather more extreme. This means stronger hurricanes, longer droughts, more intense heat waves, and heavier flooding. These extreme weather events can damage homes, affect food supplies, and harm ecosystems.

pages.title: Renewable Energy
pages.description: Discover clean energy sources that can help save our planet.
pages.explanation: Renewable energy comes from sources that won't run out and don't pollute! Solar power uses energy from the sun, wind power uses moving air, and hydropower uses flowing water. By switching from fossil fuels to renewable energy, we can significantly reduce carbon emissions.

pages.title: What You Can Do
pages.description: Simple actions that make a big difference for our planet.
pages.explanation: Everyone can help fight climate change! You can reduce, reuse, and recycle. Walk or bike instead of driving. Turn off lights when you leave a room. Plant trees and take care of plants. Eat less meat and more vegetables. Every small action adds up to make a big difference!

pages.title: Our Future
pages.description: Imagine a world where we live in harmony with nature.
pages.explanation: The future of our planet is in our hands! By working together, we can create a world with clean air, healthy oceans, and thriving forests. Scientists, inventors, and young people like YOU are finding new ways to protect our planet every day. Together, we can make a difference!
